perturbation.original_text: For purpose of this Agreement, (a) the term “Buyer” means all of undersigned, including both the potential buyer interested in the Transaction, and such buyer’s broker , and both such b uyer and buyer’s broker are bound by the provisions of this agreement; and (b) any information provided to Buyer, or otherwise learned by Buyer, concerning the Business, Seller or Transaction shall collectively be referred to herein as “Confidential Inform ation”.
perturbation.changed_text: For purpose of this Agreement, (a) the term “Buyer” means only the potential buyer interested in the Transaction, and explicitly excludes the buyer's broker, notwithstanding any other provision of this agreement; and (b) any information provided to Buyer, or otherwise learned by Buyer, concerning the Business, Seller or Transaction shall collectively be referred to herein as “Confidential Inform ation”.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding the buyer's broker from the definition of 'Buyer', the contract attempts to limit liability and confidentiality obligations. This contradicts the standard interpretation of agency law, where a principal (buyer) can be held responsible for the actions of their agent (broker). This affects enforcement by creating ambiguity about who is bound by the confidentiality clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2330 states that a principal is responsible to third persons for the wrongful acts committed by his agent in the transaction of such business. By excluding the buyer's broker from the definition of 'Buyer', the contract attempts to circumvent this responsibility, which is a contradiction of established agency law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 2330. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. AGENCY [2295 - 2400] ( Title 9 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Agency in General [2295 - 2357] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Mutual Obligations of Principals and Third Persons [2330 - 2339] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) 2330. An agent represents his principal for all purposes within the scope of his actual or ostensible authority, and all the rights and liabilities which would accrue to the agent from transactions within such limit, if they had been entered into on his own account, accrue to the principal. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract explicitly excludes the buyer's broker from the definition of 'Buyer,' attempting to limit liability and confidentiality obligations. This contradicts California Civil Code § 2330, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that a principal is responsible for the acts of their agent within the scope of their authority. By excluding the broker, the contract attempts to circumvent this responsibility, creating a direct conflict with established agency law. The changed text directly undermines the legal obligation of the principal to be responsible for the actions of their agent, thus the change would trigger a legal violation.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer understands that Confidential Information includes, without limitation: the fact that Business is for sale; financial details; identity of suppliers and customers; and any information not generally known by public.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer understands that Confidential Information includes only: the fact that Business is for sale; financial details. All other information, including the identity of suppliers and customers, is explicitly excluded from Confidential Information and may be freely shared.
perturbation.explanation: By narrowly defining 'Confidential Information' to exclude supplier and customer identities, the contract contradicts the standard definition of trade secrets and confidential business information. This modification could impact the enforcement of confidentiality obligations, particularly regarding the protection of valuable business relationships.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Excluding customer and supplier identities directly contradicts this because they often derive independent economic value from not being generally known.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly excludes supplier and customer identities from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' allowing it to be freely shared. This directly contradicts the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), as explained in the Law Explanation. The scraped snippet defines 'Misappropriation' in CIV 3426.1 as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' which includes information that derives independent economic value. Customer and supplier identities often meet this criterion, and the modified contract actively permits their disclosure, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees to defend, indemnify, protect an d hold harmless Broker, and release Broker, in connection with all information provided to Buyer, including all Confidential Information , and in connection with any breach by Buyer of any of its obligations under this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees to defend, indemnify, protect and hold harmless Broker, but only for acts of gross negligence or willful misconduct directly attributable to the Buyer. The Buyer is not responsible for any incidental, consequential, or punitive damages.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the indemnification to gross negligence or willful misconduct and excluding certain damages, the contract significantly reduces the Broker's protection compared to standard indemnification agreements. This shift could be unenforceable if it contradicts established principles of contract law concerning indemnification.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2778 provides rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. While parties can contractually modify their indemnity obligations, limitations that undermine the essential purpose of indemnification can be challenged. Limiting the scope to gross negligence and excluding consequential damages could be interpreted as contradicting the intent of full indemnification, making it potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 2778. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reque
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits the indemnification to gross negligence or willful misconduct and excludes consequential damages. This directly weakens the protection afforded to the Broker compared to standard indemnification, as described in the Perturbation Explanation. California Civil Code § 2778 provides rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. Specifically, it states that these rules apply 'unless a contrary intention appears.' While this suggests parties can modify obligations, the Law Explanation argues that limitations that undermine the essential purpose of indemnification can be challenged. The modification arguably contradicts the principle of full indemnification implicit in the original text and the general purpose of indemnity agreements. Given the reduction in scope and coverage, the change introduces a significant alteration that would likely fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer acknowledge s and agree s that any breach of any of its/his/her obligations hereunder will cause Seller and the Business irreparable harm for which Seller and the Business have no adequate remedy at law, and that Seller and the Business shall be entitled to injunctive and other equitable relief to prevent a breach or continued breach of this agreement, in addition to any other remedies Seller and Business may have at law or in equity, and that this agreement shall be specifically enforceable in accordance with its terms.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer acknowledges and agrees that a monetary award is the sole and exclusive remedy for any breach of this agreement. Under no circumstances shall Seller and the Business be entitled to injunctive relief or specific performance.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that monetary damages are the sole remedy, the contract attempts to waive the right to injunctive relief. While parties can sometimes limit remedies, waiving injunctive relief entirely may not be enforceable, particularly in cases involving confidential information where monetary damages are difficult to quantify.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 526 outlines the circumstances in which an injunction may be granted. While freedom to contract exists, a complete waiver of injunctive relief can be challenged if it is against public policy or renders the contract unconscionable, especially when protecting trade secrets or confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 526 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 526. (a) An injunction may be granted in the following cases: (1) When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a limited period or perpetually. (2) When it appears by the complaint or affidavits that the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action. (3) When it appears, during the litigation, that a party to the action is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party to the action respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. (4) When pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. (5) Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. (6) Where the
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that monetary damages are the 'sole and exclusive remedy,' and that 'Under no circumstances shall Seller and the Business be entitled to injunctive relief or specific performance.' This directly contradicts the original text, which allowed for injunctive relief. The Law Explanation mentions that a complete waiver of injunctive relief can be challenged, particularly when protecting trade secrets or confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing CCP 526, outlines cases where an injunction may be granted, including when pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief, and where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. The changed text's complete waiver of injunctive relief directly undermines the possibility of obtaining such relief in situations outlined in CCP 526, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not re sulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover, in addition to any damages or compensation received, its costs and expenses incurred in connection with such action or proceeding, including any reasonable attorneys ’ fees, expenses and court costs.
perturbation.changed_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not resulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, each party shall bear its own costs and attorneys' fees, regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that each party bears its own costs, the contract attempts to waive the right to attorney's fees for the prevailing party, departing from the established concept of fee shifting and potentially discouraging litigation to enforce the agreement's provisions.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021 generally allows parties to contract for the payment of attorney's fees. However, such waivers must be clear and unequivocal. Eliminating attorney's fees shifts the risk of litigation and can impact the willingness of a party to enforce their rights under the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original agreement regarding attorney's fees. The original text stipulates that the prevailing party is entitled to recover costs and attorney's fees, while the changed text explicitly states that each party bears its own costs and attorney's fees, regardless of the outcome. This eliminates the established concept of fee shifting. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1021, states that 'Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties...'. This suggests the parties can agree to attorney's fees, and the original agreement did so, but the modified version negates this agreement. Thus, the changed text creates a legal contradiction by directly reversing the original agreement concerning attorney's fees. This falls under the definition of Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction because the contractual language deviates from the original, agreed-upon terms for attorney's fees.
